question: Explain the concept of stationarity in time series analysis. Why is it a fundamental assumption for many models, and what are the consequences of modeling a non-stationary series without first transforming it?
expectedPoints: Definition of stationarity: constant mean, constant variance, and autocovariance depends only on lag (not time).
It is a key assumption for AR, MA, and ARMA models to ensure stable parameters and valid statistical inference.
Consequences include spurious regression (finding relationships where none exist), unreliable coefficient estimates, and poor forecasting performance.

question: Describe the purpose of the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) and Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF) plots. How would you use them to identify the order of an AR(p) or MA(q) process?
expectedPoints: ACF plot measures the correlation between observations at different lags, showing the overall dependency structure.
PACF plot measures the correlation between observations at a specific lag, controlling for correlations at shorter lags, revealing the direct effect.

For an AR(p) process: ACF decays gradually (tails off), PACF has significant spikes up to lag p then cuts off. For an MA(q) process: ACF has significant spikes up to lag q then cuts off, PACF decays gradually.

question: What is the main difference between an autoregressive (AR) model and a moving average (MA) model in terms of what they represent?
expectedPoints: An AR model expresses the current value of the series as a linear combination of its own past values plus a shock.
An MA model expresses the current value of the series as a linear combination of past forecast errors (shocks).
An AR model captures momentum and persistence, while an MA model captures the effect of transient shocks on the series.
